fr.elements: Main Building; Landscaped Area; Perimeter Fencing; Roof System
fr.recommendations: Ensuring that paths through the landscaped areas remain clear and free of dense vegetation could improve emergency responder access and movement throughout the property. Creating multiple points of access, especially toward the rear of the property, may facilitate more efficient navigation and operational support for emergency personnel. Additionally, maintaining the perimeter fencing in a manner that allows visibility and ease of entry where necessary could enhance quick entry and exit for responders. Evaluating and adjusting the distribution and thickness of the vegetation near main access points could assist in improving visibility and access, allowing emergency services to stage and deploy equipment more effectively during a response event.

wildfire.conditions: Dense Tree Canopy; Vegetation Adjacent to Structure; Scattered Trees; Vegetated Perimeter; Tree Cluster; Vegetation Adjacent to Fence; Open Terrain; Limited Defensible Space
wildfire.recommendations: Enhancing defensible space can be achieved by creating greater separation between the dense tree canopy and the structure, which would help reduce fuel continuity. Managing vegetation adjacent to the structure and fence is critical; trimming or selectively removing plants could reduce potential fire exposure to these areas. Thinning the tree cluster and scattered trees can minimize potential fuel concentrations, contributing to a more effective defensible space. Expanding open terrain and maintaining it as a fuel break will further help interrupt fire paths. Maintaining a clearer space around the perimeter of the property and within immediate proximity to structures can support both firefighter access and suppression operations. Consistent vegetation management practices will promote long-term wildfire resilience, ensuring a prepared property when faced with wildfire conditions.
